content.value: Barkpark is a headless CMS — one content model, many surfaces — whose marquee feature is a first-class content graph that we benchmark against Obsidian's own graph view.

Barkpark stores content once as a typed Document against a Schema, then projects it onto every surface that needs it. There is no surface-specific data model: the Go TUI, the bp CLI, the Phoenix API, LiveView Studio, the JS SDK, and the Web Demo all read the same content layer. What each surface can do is taught at runtime by the Capabilities Manifest.
Behaviour and data extensions ride a public

highway via the Plugin Behaviour — plugins add data and behaviour, never UI. The host is generic enough that OnixEdit, Bulldocs, the Tasks Plugin, the Media Plugin, and the Frt Plugin are all guests, not special cases. The test of correctness is the Fresh Install Invariant: with every plugin disabled, Barkpark still works.

Documents link to documents through a Reference Field, and the Edge Extractor projects those links into a Content Edges Table. That graph — the dual of the Indx Retriever Seam — drives the Graph View and powers Blast Radius analysis. Rendering it well is why this whole vault exists; see Graph Comparison, introduced from Welcome.

documents.body:
## One content model, every surface

Barkpark is an open-source headless CMS built around a single idea: your content should outlive any one frontend. You model it once — a typed, versioned schema served from `GET /v1/capabilities` — and consume it everywhere. A Go TUI and the `bp` CLI for the terminal, a Phoenix-backed API and LiveView Studio for editors, a JavaScript SDK for application teams, and a Next.js web demo that proves the whole loop ships.

## Why we built it

Most CMSes make you choose between editor ergonomics and developer control. Barkpark refuses the trade. Editors get a real-time Studio with structured fields, drafts, and publishing workflow. Developers get a clean HTTP contract, webhooks for cache revalidation, and an SDK that reads production with a single token. The schema is the API — change a field type and every surface updates in lock-step.

## Plugins that ride the host

Everything beyond the core content model is a plugin on the `Barkpark.Plugin` behaviour — Media for assets, Bulldocs for long-form documents, Tasks for the work queue, ONIX export for publishing metadata, and the Indx retriever seam for semantic search. The fresh-install invariant is our test of correctness: turn every plugin off and Barkpark still runs, still serves content, still passes its smoke tests. Plugins add data and behaviour, never UI lock-in.

## Search and the content graph

Barkpark treats your content as a graph, not a pile of rows. Every reference between documents becomes an edge, so search results carry their relationships with them — an author links to their posts, a page links to its hero asset, a project links to the case study behind it. The result is a site that reads like a real, connected publication instead of disconnected records.

## Open source, production-grade

Barkpark runs in production on commodity ARM64 hardware with a single binary and a systemd unit. No vendor lock-in, no per-seat tax, no surprise egress bills. Read the [docs](barkpark), explore the [schema model](schema), or wire up the [JS SDK](web-demo) and ship today.

documents.description:
## barkpark.cloud — the managed home for Barkpark

barkpark.cloud is the hosted control plane that turns the open-source Barkpark headless CMS into a zero-ops product. The brief was blunt: take the same single binary we ship to self-hosters and run it so reliably that a two-person editorial team never thinks about servers again.

### The challenge

Barkpark deploys as one Go binary plus a Phoenix release on ARM64 — beautifully simple for a hobbyist, deceptively involved at fleet scale. We needed multi-tenant isolation without forking the core, automated TLS and domain cutovers, webhook delivery with cache revalidation that survives restarts, and a Studio that stays click-alive through every deploy.

### What we built

We stood up barkpark.cloud on commodity Hetzner ARM64 hardware behind a managed load balancer, each tenant on an isolated dataset with its own schema. A `make domain-cutover` workflow handles DNS, TLS, and the Phoenix `check_origin` rewrite in one motion — the same cutover that once silently killed Studio LiveView now ships in a single command. Backups are continuous, upgrades are blue-green, and the whole fleet is observable from one dashboard.

### The result

Cloud customers get hosted Studio, durable webhook delivery, full-text and semantic search via the Indx engine, and graph-aware ranking — with no servers to patch and no egress invoices to decode. The same binary, the same content model, now run for you.

blocks.text: Point an AI agent at it

content.value: 1. Token. Any bearer token works for the task endpoints (read tier — claim/close are workflow ops, not document mutations). Creating tasks goes through the mutate endpoint, which needs an admin token. Dev default: barkpark-dev-token.
2. Discover. One call teaches the whole surface:

blocks.value: bp capabilities -o json          # or: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" $API/v1/capabilities

content.value: 3. Create tasks. Tasks are documents — create them through the standard mutation envelope. Required content: kind: "task" and a valid lifecycle_status. Optional: priority (0–4, 0 = highest), assignee, parent_id, labels, papers, plus the dossier fields (description, design, design_doc paper slug, acceptance_criteria list, estimate, due_at, outcome, …) — the schema (GET /v1/schemas/:dataset, name task) is the authoritative field list.

blocks.value:
curl -X POST $API/v1/data/mutate/production \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"mutations":[{"create":{"_type":"task","_id":"t1","title":"Ship the docs",
       "content":{"kind":"task","lifecycle_status":"open","priority":1}}}]}'

content.value: Draft prefix note: create always lands as a draft (stored id drafts.t1). The task endpoints resolve the bare id t1 automatically — you can use either t1 or drafts.t1. If both a published t1 and a draft drafts.t1 exist, t1 matches the published row (exact match wins). Task lifecycle is independent of draft/publish; publishing a task is optional.
4. The claim → work → close loop. Pick a stable worker_id per agent.

blocks.value:
# Queue claim: atomically take the NEXT ready task (priority ASC, then oldest)
bp task next agent-1                # prints the claimed doc_id + epoch; no_ready on empty queue
curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/claim \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"worker_id":"agent-1"}'
# → {"ok":true,"doc":{...,"claim":{"worker":"agent-1","ts_iso":"…","epoch":1}}}
# → {"ok":false,"reason":"no_ready"} when the queue is empty

# Targeted claim: name the row
bp task claim t1 agent-1            # args: <doc_id> <worker_id>

# ... do the work ...

# Close: CAS on the fencing epoch you were handed at claim time
bp task close t1 agent-1 1          # args: <doc_id> <worker_id> <observed_epoch> [lifecycle_status]

items.value: Claim flips lifecycle_status to in_progress and stamps content.claim = {worker, ts_iso, epoch}. The epoch bumps on every claim (losing a race → 409 stale_claim).

Close requires worker_id + observed_epoch (string ints accepted). Epoch mismatch → 409 fenced_off — the only protection against a stale-but-alive worker writing after its lease was swept. Optional body: lifecycle_status (done | cancelled | blocked, default done) and observed_rev.
Leases expire. A sweeper runs every minute; claims idle past task_lease_ttl_seconds (default 300) are released and emit task.lease_expired. Finish or re-claim.

Ready means: lifecycle_status ∈ {open, blocked} and every outbound blocks edge points at a done task. Closing a task done auto-flips dependents from blocked → open when their full blocker set is done.

blocks.value:
# t2 waits on t1 (from = dependent, to = blocker; kind defaults to "blocks")
curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/edges -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"from_id":"drafts.t2","to_id":"drafts.t1"}'

curl $API/v1/tasks/drafts.t2/edges -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   # ?kind=all for every edge kind

curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/drafts.t1/labels -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"add":["sprint-3"],"remove":[]}'

# Link a paper (a Bulldocs doc) to a task — design notes travel with the work
curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/drafts.t1/papers -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"add":["design-notes"]}'

bp task ready --limit 5                          # the unblocked queue
bp task ls --limit 20                            # everything, goals included
curl "$API/v1/tasks?lifecycle_status=open&label=sprint-3" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"
curl "$API/v1/tasks?parent=drafts.g1" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   # a goal's rail, oldest first

content.value: Filters: kind, lifecycle_status, phase_id, parent, label, type, limit (index default 1000; ready and the bp verbs default 50).
7. Watch the stream. Subscribe to /v1/data/listen/:dataset (SSE) and react to task.claimed, task.closed, task.lease_expired, etc. — no polling.

body.html:
NeYoo

Tasks are plain type:task documents — no second store, no sync. A root task is a goal; children nest under it; dependencies form a graph; claims are atomic and fenced so many workers can't trample each other.

What you get

Barkpark as your AI's task board: the agent claims work over HTTP, you steer the same queue in the browser Studio, and every change broadcasts to both sides in real time.

What you get

SurfaceWhat
Studio Tasks paneA Tasks ✅ desk group at /studio (plugin desk item), with lifecycle tabs (open · in_progress · blocked · closed · all). The editor is a full dossier in four groups — brief (description, design notes, an expandable design_doc paper reference, checkable acceptance criteria with met/evidence), work (priority, assignee, estimate, due date, labels), close (outcome, reason), system — plus soft validations (e.g. closing done without an outcome warns). dependencies and claim render read-only ("managed via API").
bp verbsbp task ls / ready / get / next / claim / close — manifest-driven from GET /v1/capabilities, provenance plugin:tasks.
Terminal TUITask lists carry quick actions: c claims the highlighted task, x closes it (same fenced endpoints; worker id from BARKPARK_WORKER_ID, default tui-<hostname>). Plus the standard desk keys — / search, n new, y duplicate, D×2 delete.
HTTP APIEleven bearer-token endpoints under /v1/tasks/* (read tier, not admin): list, ready-queue, prime (one-call agent rehydration), queue claim, targeted claim, close, fetch-with-children, edges, labels, paper links.
EventsEvery task op emits a mutation_events row — task.claimed / task.closed / task.mutated / task.relabeled / task.referenced / task.lease_expired — streamed over SSE at /v1/data/listen/:dataset.

Lifecycle states: open · in_progress · blocked · done · cancelled.

Set up from zero

curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/FRIKKern/barkpark/main/scripts/install-cli.sh | sh
bp            # no config + TTY → the setup wizard, then the TUI

The wizard's clean profile pre-checks the bulldocs and tasks plugins (the server unions media in on its own). Accept, and the task schema, routes, and cron workers are live on first boot. Already running a dev server on :4000? It blocks the local target's DB reset — stop it first, or pick connect instead.

Existing installs — enable via env and restart:

BARKPARK_PLUGINS=bulldocs,tasks    # CSV whitelist · unset = all plugins · empty = kill switch
  • Server (deploy.sh / bp setup --target deploy): pass BARKPARK_PLUGINS=bulldocs,tasks in the deploy env — it persists into /opt/barkpark/.env, sourced by api/start.sh (the systemd unit's ExecStart).
  • Docker: docker-compose.yml passes the variable through as a bare - BARKPARK_PLUGINS entry — export it in the invoking shell before docker compose up.
  • Local mix: BARKPARK_PLUGINS=bulldocs,tasks mix phx.server.

The task schema auto-registers on every boot (idempotent on (name, dataset)); two Oban cron jobs come with it — a lease sweeper every minute and compaction every six hours.

Point an AI agent at it

1. Token. Any bearer token works for the task endpoints (read tier — claim/close are workflow ops, not document mutations). Creating tasks goes through the mutate endpoint, which needs an admin token. Dev default: barkpark-dev-token.

2. Discover. One call teaches the whole surface:

bp capabilities -o json          # or: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" $API/v1/capabilities

3. Create tasks. Tasks are documents — create them through the standard mutation envelope. Required content: kind: "task" and a valid lifecycle_status. Optional: priority (0–4, 0 = highest), assignee, parent_id, labels, papers, plus the dossier fields (description, design, design_doc paper slug, acceptance_criteria list, estimate, due_at, outcome, …) — the schema (GET /v1/schemas/:dataset, name task) is the authoritative field list.

curl -X POST $API/v1/data/mutate/production \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"mutations":[{"create":{"_type":"task","_id":"t1","title":"Ship the docs",
       "content":{"kind":"task","lifecycle_status":"open","priority":1}}}]}'
Draft prefix note: create always lands as a draft (stored id drafts.t1). The task endpoints resolve the bare id t1 automatically — you can use either t1 or drafts.t1. If both a published t1 and a draft drafts.t1 exist, t1 matches the published row (exact match wins). Task lifecycle is independent of draft/publish; publishing a task is optional.

4. The claim → work → close loop. Pick a stable worker_id per agent.

# Queue claim: atomically take the NEXT ready task (priority ASC, then oldest)
bp task next agent-1                # prints the claimed doc_id + epoch; no_ready on empty queue
curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/claim \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"worker_id":"agent-1"}'
# → {"ok":true,"doc":{...,"claim":{"worker":"agent-1","ts_iso":"…","epoch":1}}}
# → {"ok":false,"reason":"no_ready"} when the queue is empty

# Targeted claim: name the row
bp task claim t1 agent-1            # args: <doc_id> <worker_id>

# ... do the work ...

# Close: CAS on the fencing epoch you were handed at claim time
bp task close t1 agent-1 1          # args: <doc_id> <worker_id> <observed_epoch> [lifecycle_status]

The contract, precisely:

  • Claim flips lifecycle_status to in_progress and stamps content.claim = {worker, ts_iso, epoch}. The epoch bumps on every claim (losing a race → 409 stale_claim).
  • Close requires worker_id + observed_epoch (string ints accepted). Epoch mismatch → 409 fenced_off — the only protection against a stale-but-alive worker writing after its lease was swept. Optional body: lifecycle_status (done | cancelled | blocked, default done) and observed_rev.
  • Leases expire. A sweeper runs every minute; claims idle past task_lease_ttl_seconds (default 300) are released and emit task.lease_expired. Finish or re-claim.
  • Ready means: lifecycle_status ∈ {open, blocked} and every outbound blocks edge points at a done task. Closing a task done auto-flips dependents from blocked → open when their full blocker set is done.

5. Dependencies, labels, papers.

# t2 waits on t1 (from = dependent, to = blocker; kind defaults to "blocks")
curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/edges -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"from_id":"drafts.t2","to_id":"drafts.t1"}'

curl $API/v1/tasks/drafts.t2/edges -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   # ?kind=all for every edge kind

curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/drafts.t1/labels -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"add":["sprint-3"],"remove":[]}'

# Link a paper (a Bulldocs doc) to a task — design notes travel with the work
curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/drafts.t1/papers -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"add":["design-notes"]}'

6. Filtered reads.

bp task ready --limit 5                          # the unblocked queue
bp task ls --limit 20                            # everything, goals included
curl "$API/v1/tasks?lifecycle_status=open&label=sprint-3" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"
curl "$API/v1/tasks?parent=drafts.g1" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   # a goal's rail, oldest first

Filters: kind, lifecycle_status, phase_id, parent, label, type, limit (index default 1000; ready and the bp verbs default 50).

7. Watch the stream. Subscribe to /v1/data/listen/:dataset (SSE) and react to task.claimed, task.closed, task.lease_expired, etc. — no polling.

Working with your AI in Studio

Open /studio → the Tasks ✅ group. The division of labour:

You (Studio form)The agent (API)
Flip lifecycle_status (5-state dropdown), set priority, assignee, edit titles/descriptionsclaim / close with fencing, add edges, relabel, link papers
Triage: open new tasks, cancel dead onesDrain the ready queue in priority order

The terminal TUI edits the flat fields (title, description, lifecycle, priority, …) and carries the claim/close quick actions (c / x on a task list or in a task's editor — same fenced endpoints the agent uses); composite dossier fields like acceptance_criteria are Studio- and API-editable only (the documented v1 TUI constraint). dependencies and claim show as read-only pretty-printed JSON in the editor — round-tripping structured values through a text input would corrupt them, so the form simply never submits those fields and the API stays the single writer. Everything updates live via PubSub: when the agent claims a task, your pane reflects it without a refresh.

Goals and phases

Everything is a task. The pattern:

  • Goal = a root task (no parent_id).
  • Phase / subtask = a task whose content.parent_id is the parent's doc id.
  • Rail = a task's chronological children: GET /v1/tasks?parent=<id> (oldest first), and GET /v1/tasks/:id returns one level of children summaries inline (doc_id, title, lifecycle_status) plus child_count.
  • Scope a worker to one phase: POST /v1/tasks/claim with {"worker_id":"agent-1","phase_id":"<phase-doc-id>"}, or GET /v1/tasks/ready?phase_id=….

Goal → phases → leaf tasks, with blocks edges sequencing the phases, gives you a multi-phase project board in a standard Barkpark install.

Workspaces, projects, datasets — experiment without mess

Spin up an isolated sandbox in one command — experiments stay fully isolated from your real work (deleting a whole workspace is on the deferred ledger; today spikes are abandoned in place, never leaked). Any write-tier token may create a workspace — it arrives ready to use:

bp workspace create Spike
# → workspace + you as owner-member + a Default project + a production dataset
#   (slug derived from the name; explicit: --slug spike)

bp -w spike workspace project-create agents-v2    # member-gated; -w names the workspace
bp workspace ls                                   # what your token can reach
# Raw HTTP equivalents: POST /api/workspaces {"name":…} and
# POST /api/workspaces/:slug/projects {"name":…} — see docs/cheatsheets/http-api.md

Scoped Studio lives at /w/:workspace_slug/p/:project_slug/studio; scoped data routes mirror under the same prefix. The flat /v1/tasks/* surface operates on the server's default (unscoped) scope — under scoped pipelines, task reads and claims are strictly filtered by workspace + project. Membership is the boundary: non-members get 404, never an existence leak.

Troubleshooting

SymptomCause → fix
No Tasks pane in StudioPlugin not whitelisted (BARKPARK_PLUGINS set without tasks) or the task schema isn't registered in this dataset. Fix env, restart; the schema auto-registers on boot.
404 on /v1/tasks/*Plugin disabled — routes only mount when the tasks plugin is registered.
404 task not found on an id you just createdRare — the endpoints resolve bare t1 to drafts.t1 automatically. Check that the task plugin is enabled and the token has access.
400 worker_id is requiredClaim/close need worker_id — positional via bp (bp task claim <id> <worker>), JSON body via curl.
409 fenced_offYour observed_epoch is stale — the lease was swept and re-claimed. Re-claim, then close with the new epoch.
409 stale_claimLost a concurrent claim race. Call /v1/tasks/claim again.
409 not_readyTargeted claim on a task that is in_progress/done/cancelled.
409 blocked_by_unsatisfied_depsTargeted claim while a blocks edge points at a non-done task.
{"ok":false,"reason":"no_ready"}Not an error — the queue is empty (HTTP 200).
Task invisible in Studio but in APITenancy: the doc carries a different workspace/project scope than the Studio you're looking at.

NeYoo

Tasks are plain type:task documents — no second store, no sync. A root task is a goal; children nest under it; dependencies form a graph; claims are atomic and fenced so many workers can't trample each other.

What you get

Barkpark as your AI's task board: the agent claims work over HTTP, you steer the same queue in the browser Studio, and every change broadcasts to both sides in real time.

What you get

SurfaceWhat
Studio Tasks paneA Tasks ✅ desk group at /studio (plugin desk item), with lifecycle tabs (open · in_progress · blocked · closed · all). The editor is a full dossier in four groups — brief (description, design notes, an expandable design_doc paper reference, checkable acceptance criteria with met/evidence), work (priority, assignee, estimate, due date, labels), close (outcome, reason), system — plus soft validations (e.g. closing done without an outcome warns). dependencies and claim render read-only ("managed via API").
bp verbsbp task ls / ready / get / next / claim / close — manifest-driven from GET /v1/capabilities, provenance plugin:tasks.
Terminal TUITask lists carry quick actions: c claims the highlighted task, x closes it (same fenced endpoints; worker id from BARKPARK_WORKER_ID, default tui-<hostname>). Plus the standard desk keys — / search, n new, y duplicate, D×2 delete.
HTTP APIEleven bearer-token endpoints under /v1/tasks/* (read tier, not admin): list, ready-queue, prime (one-call agent rehydration), queue claim, targeted claim, close, fetch-with-children, edges, labels, paper links.
EventsEvery task op emits a mutation_events row — task.claimed / task.closed / task.mutated / task.relabeled / task.referenced / task.lease_expired — streamed over SSE at /v1/data/listen/:dataset.

Lifecycle states: open · in_progress · blocked · done · cancelled.

Set up from zero

curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/FRIKKern/barkpark/main/scripts/install-cli.sh | sh
bp            # no config + TTY → the setup wizard, then the TUI

The wizard's clean profile pre-checks the bulldocs and tasks plugins (the server unions media in on its own). Accept, and the task schema, routes, and cron workers are live on first boot. Already running a dev server on :4000? It blocks the local target's DB reset — stop it first, or pick connect instead.

Existing installs — enable via env and restart:

BARKPARK_PLUGINS=bulldocs,tasks    # CSV whitelist · unset = all plugins · empty = kill switch
  • Server (deploy.sh / bp setup --target deploy): pass BARKPARK_PLUGINS=bulldocs,tasks in the deploy env — it persists into /opt/barkpark/.env, sourced by api/start.sh (the systemd unit's ExecStart).
  • Docker: docker-compose.yml passes the variable through as a bare - BARKPARK_PLUGINS entry — export it in the invoking shell before docker compose up.
  • Local mix: BARKPARK_PLUGINS=bulldocs,tasks mix phx.server.

The task schema auto-registers on every boot (idempotent on (name, dataset)); two Oban cron jobs come with it — a lease sweeper every minute and compaction every six hours.

Point an AI agent at it

1. Token. Any bearer token works for the task endpoints (read tier — claim/close are workflow ops, not document mutations). Creating tasks goes through the mutate endpoint, which needs an admin token. Dev default: barkpark-dev-token.

2. Discover. One call teaches the whole surface:

bp capabilities -o json          # or: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" $API/v1/capabilities

3. Create tasks. Tasks are documents — create them through the standard mutation envelope. Required content: kind: "task" and a valid lifecycle_status. Optional: priority (0–4, 0 = highest), assignee, parent_id, labels, papers, plus the dossier fields (description, design, design_doc paper slug, acceptance_criteria list, estimate, due_at, outcome, …) — the schema (GET /v1/schemas/:dataset, name task) is the authoritative field list.

curl -X POST $API/v1/data/mutate/production \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"mutations":[{"create":{"_type":"task","_id":"t1","title":"Ship the docs",
       "content":{"kind":"task","lifecycle_status":"open","priority":1}}}]}'
Draft prefix note: create always lands as a draft (stored id drafts.t1). The task endpoints resolve the bare id t1 automatically — you can use either t1 or drafts.t1. If both a published t1 and a draft drafts.t1 exist, t1 matches the published row (exact match wins). Task lifecycle is independent of draft/publish; publishing a task is optional.

4. The claim → work → close loop. Pick a stable worker_id per agent.

# Queue claim: atomically take the NEXT ready task (priority ASC, then oldest)
bp task next agent-1                # prints the claimed doc_id + epoch; no_ready on empty queue
curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/claim \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"worker_id":"agent-1"}'
# → {"ok":true,"doc":{...,"claim":{"worker":"agent-1","ts_iso":"…","epoch":1}}}
# → {"ok":false,"reason":"no_ready"} when the queue is empty

# Targeted claim: name the row
bp task claim t1 agent-1            # args: <doc_id> <worker_id>

# ... do the work ...

# Close: CAS on the fencing epoch you were handed at claim time
bp task close t1 agent-1 1          # args: <doc_id> <worker_id> <observed_epoch> [lifecycle_status]

The contract, precisely:

  • Claim flips lifecycle_status to in_progress and stamps content.claim = {worker, ts_iso, epoch}. The epoch bumps on every claim (losing a race → 409 stale_claim).
  • Close requires worker_id + observed_epoch (string ints accepted). Epoch mismatch → 409 fenced_off — the only protection against a stale-but-alive worker writing after its lease was swept. Optional body: lifecycle_status (done | cancelled | blocked, default done) and observed_rev.
  • Leases expire. A sweeper runs every minute; claims idle past task_lease_ttl_seconds (default 300) are released and emit task.lease_expired. Finish or re-claim.
  • Ready means: lifecycle_status ∈ {open, blocked} and every outbound blocks edge points at a done task. Closing a task done auto-flips dependents from blocked → open when their full blocker set is done.

5. Dependencies, labels, papers.

# t2 waits on t1 (from = dependent, to = blocker; kind defaults to "blocks")
curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/edges -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"from_id":"drafts.t2","to_id":"drafts.t1"}'

curl $API/v1/tasks/drafts.t2/edges -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   # ?kind=all for every edge kind

curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/drafts.t1/labels -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"add":["sprint-3"],"remove":[]}'

# Link a paper (a Bulldocs doc) to a task — design notes travel with the work
curl -X POST $API/v1/tasks/drafts.t1/papers -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"add":["design-notes"]}'

6. Filtered reads.

bp task ready --limit 5                          # the unblocked queue
bp task ls --limit 20                            # everything, goals included
curl "$API/v1/tasks?lifecycle_status=open&label=sprint-3" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"
curl "$API/v1/tasks?parent=drafts.g1" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   # a goal's rail, oldest first

Filters: kind, lifecycle_status, phase_id, parent, label, type, limit (index default 1000; ready and the bp verbs default 50).

7. Watch the stream. Subscribe to /v1/data/listen/:dataset (SSE) and react to task.claimed, task.closed, task.lease_expired, etc. — no polling.

# Pseudocode for WI3 — illustrative, not for paste
Req.new(
  retry: :transient,                # retry on network errors + 408/429/5xx
  max_retries: 5,                   # 5 attempts ≈ ~62s ceiling at 2^n
  retry_delay: fn attempt ->
    base_ms = min(60_000, :math.pow(2, attempt) * 1_000 |> trunc())
    jitter_ms = :rand.uniform(div(base_ms, 4))   # up to 25% jitter
    base_ms + jitter_ms
  end,
  retry_log_level: :warning
)

content.value: Welcome — this vault is a comparison fixture for Barkpark's graph view versus Obsidian's.

This Obsidian vault documents the Barkpark headless CMS as a densely-interlinked note web on purpose: it is the side-by-side fixture for comparing Barkpark's own Graph View against Obsidian's graph view. Start at Barkpark for the front door and the maps of content — Surfaces (how you touch it), Content Model (the data layer), and Content Graph (the marquee feature) — then open Graph Comparison, the working checklist of Obsidian's reference graph parameters mapped to Barkpark's Graph Renderer, which is the whole reason this vault exists.
Plugins extend Barkpark with data and behaviour — never UI — by riding the public `Barkpark.*` highway, and the host works fully with all of them off.

Every plugin implements the Plugin Behaviour: a documented Elixir behaviour that grants schemas, endpoints, and background work, but no bespoke UI. When a plugin needs an affordance the host lacks, the rule is to extend the host generically rather than smuggle in a one-off interface. The guarantee this buys is the Fresh Install Invariant — disable every plugin and Barkpark still serves content.

## Pricing that scales with you, not against you

Barkpark is open source and free to self-host, forever. The paid tiers exist for teams who would rather we run the cluster, watch the dashboards, and answer the pager at 3 a.m. No per-seat pricing — invite your whole editorial team without doing arithmetic.

## Community — $0

The full Barkpark, self-hosted. Unlimited content types, unlimited documents, unlimited editors. The Go TUI, the `bp` CLI, the Phoenix API, LiveView Studio, the JavaScript SDK, and every first-party plugin — Media, Bulldocs, Tasks, ONIX, and the Indx retriever seam. You bring the server; we bring the binary. Community support via GitHub and Discord.

## Cloud — $49 / month

Managed Barkpark on our infrastructure. We handle deploys, backups, TLS, and upgrades. Includes the hosted Studio, webhook delivery with cache revalidation, search powered by the content graph, and the Indx semantic engine. Generous usage limits that fit most production sites, with transparent overage pricing and no egress surprises. Email support with a one-business-day response.

## Enterprise — let's talk

For publishers and platforms running Barkpark at scale. Dedicated capacity, SSO, audit logging, custom plugins on the `Barkpark.Plugin` behaviour, ONIX/Bokbasen export pipelines, and a named support engineer. SLAs on uptime and response. Volume discounts and annual contracts.

## Frequently asked

**Is the open-source version really complete?** Yes. Cloud and Enterprise add operations and support, not features locked behind a paywall. The fresh-install invariant guarantees the core works on its own.

**Can I move between tiers?** Self-host today, migrate to Cloud later, or export your dataset and walk away — your content is portable [documents](document) in standard formats, never trapped.

**What about search?** Every tier ships full-text [search](search); Cloud and Enterprise add the [Indx](indx-engine) semantic engine and graph-aware ranking out of the box.

The surfaces are the many faces of Barkpark's one content model — each a different way to read, write, and render the same documents.
One binary, the Go program, hosts both the Go TUI (an interactive terminal client) and the bp CLI (scriptable commands). Both are manifest-driven — they self-configure from
, the Capabilities Manifest — so a new server feature appears in the CLI without a recompile.

The Phoenix API is the server itself: the Elixir application that owns the content layer and serves the HTTP API v1. Inside it, LiveView Studio is the editing UI — a server-rendered admin surface for authoring a Document against its Schema.
On the consumer side, the JS SDK is the typed client library, and the Web Demo (a Next.js app) is the reference site built on top of it, demonstrating Cache Revalidation against live content.
Finally, pdrender is the terminal renderer for PortableDoc blocks — the INK-like Go engine that paints rich documents inside the Go TUI.

Surfaces never hardcode the content model. The Capabilities Manifest is the contract that lets the TUI and CLI discover schemas, datasets, and plugin endpoints at runtime, so every surface stays in lockstep with Barkpark without a coordinated release.
The `Barkpark.Plugin` behaviour is the public highway every plugin rides — data and behaviour only, never UI.
behaviour and talks to the host exclusively through the documented
API surface. The rule is strict: plugins contribute
data and behaviour only, never UI

. When a plugin needs an affordance the host lacks, the host is extended *generically* so every surface benefits — this is the bidirectional-enhancement contract. Plugins never paint their own widgets into LiveView Studio or the Web Demo.

This keeps the host and its plugins loosely coupled. OnixEdit, Bulldocs, the Tasks plugin, the Media plugin, and the Frt plugin all sit behind the same highway, and each one is just data + behaviour layered onto core Documents and the Content Model. The host that owns them all is the Phoenix API. Even the Indx engine — wired through the retriever seam rather than the registry — shares this family's off-by-default discipline.

The behaviour's correctness is proven by the Fresh Install Invariant: with every plugin switched off, Barkpark still works — the Phoenix API serves, the bp CLI queries, and core edge emission keeps running. Anything a plugin breaks when disabled is a contract violation.
Because the Content Graph is a first-class core feature, each plugin *may* implement an Edge Extractor — a hook that surfaces relationships hidden inside its own payloads (a task's
edge, a media reference) so the Edge Projector can fold them into the shared Content Edges Table. Core Reference Field edges always flow regardless; plugin extractors are pure bonus.
OnixEdit exports book metadata in ONIX format for Bokbasen, the Norwegian book-trade hub.
OnixEdit turns Barkpark Documents that describe books into
— the international XML standard for book-trade metadata — and ships it to

. It is data + behaviour only: it reads the book's fields from the Content Model and emits a compliant ONIX message, without ever painting its own UI. Editing happens through ordinary Schema v2 fields in LiveView Studio.
Like every plugin, OnixEdit rides the Plugin Behaviour and talks to the host through the
API. It reads structured Document data and any Reference Fields (contributor, imprint, series) and maps them onto ONIX codelists.
OnixEdit's codelists — the controlled vocabularies that make ONIX valid —

. They are part of the plugin's own data, not borrowed from a running database, so the Fresh Install Invariant holds: with OnixEdit off, Barkpark still works; with it on after a clean install, exports validate immediately. A plugin that depended on hand-seeded codelists would fail that test. The plugin loads inside the Phoenix API alongside its sibling Frt plugin and the rest of the family.
The server-rendered editing UI built on Phoenix LiveView — and the home of Barkpark's Graph View.

LiveView Studio is the browser editing surface for Barkpark, rendered server-side by Phoenix LiveView and hosted inside the Phoenix API app. It is the richest of the Surfaces for editors: list and edit a Document, inspect its Schema, and manage Plugins content, all without a separate front-end build.

Studio hosts the Graph View pane — Barkpark's own answer to an Obsidian-style graph — which renders the Content Graph from the Content Edges Table. Selecting a node surfaces its Blast Radius: every Document that would be affected through Reference Field links, including Phantom Node and Dangling Reference markers for broken targets. This is the in-app counterpart studied in the Graph Comparison.
Because Studio is LiveView, it depends on a live WebSocket to its server. Its JS client must load, and
must match the public hostname and scheme. When
drifts from the real origin after a TLS cutover,
returns 403, the socket silently drops, and Studio becomes click-dead while still rendering HTML — a documented Production failure mode.

In prod, Studio is served by the same systemd Service as the Phoenix API, behind Caddy for TLS, and survives a Blue Green Rebuild alongside the rest of the app. An edit committed here is the upstream trigger of the freshness path: it fires the webhooks that consumers built on the JS SDK subscribe to, so changes made in Studio ripple out to downstream caches.
Frt is a content plugin riding the standard plugin highway — and it's off-by-default-safe.

Frt extends Barkpark with content behaviour over ordinary Documents. Like its siblings — OnixEdit, Bulldocs, the Tasks plugin, the Media plugin — it implements the Plugin Behaviour and talks to the host only through the
API: data and behaviour, never UI. Whatever fields and validation Frt adds, they ride on the shared Content Model and Schema v2 machinery.

Frt is a textbook citizen of the Fresh Install Invariant: with the plugin disabled, Barkpark still serves through the Phoenix API, the bp CLI still queries, and core Reference Field edges still feed the Content Graph. Nothing in core depends on Frt being present — disabling it is a no-op for the host.
